name: pods
context: Create, inspect, debug, and manage Kubernetes Pods — the smallest deployable unit in a cluster
info: Pods are the atomic unit of deployment in Kubernetes. A Pod encapsulates one or more containers that share the same network namespace (localhost), storage volumes, and lifecycle. Agents interact with Pods to deploy containers, read logs, exec into running containers for debugging, port-forward for local access, and diagnose failures like CrashLoopBackOff and OOMKilled. Pods are rarely created directly — they are managed by higher-level controllers like [[kubernetes/deployments]] and [[kubernetes/jobs]]. This skill covers Pod specs, multi-container patterns (sidecar, init containers), resource requests/limits, probes (liveness, readiness, startup), and the full debugging workflow.
body:
# Pods

A Pod is the smallest deployable unit in Kubernetes — one or more containers sharing network, storage, and a lifecycle. Pods run on worker nodes, scheduled by the Kubernetes scheduler.

## Pod Spec

```yaml
apiVersion: v1
kind: Pod
metadata:
  name: my-app
  namespace: default
  labels:
    app: my-app
    version: v1
spec:
  containers:
    - name: app
      image: myregistry/my-app:v1.2.0
      ports:
        - containerPort: 8080
          name: http
      env:
        - name: DATABASE_URL
          valueFrom:
            secretKeyRef:
              name: db-secret
              key: url
      resources:
        requests:
          cpu: 100m
          memory: 128Mi
        limits:
          cpu: 500m
          memory: 512Mi
      livenessProbe:
        httpGet:
          path: /healthz
          port: 8080
        initialDelaySeconds: 10
        periodSeconds: 15
      readinessProbe:
        httpGet:
          path: /ready
          port: 8080
        initialDelaySeconds: 5
        periodSeconds: 10
  restartPolicy: Always
```

## Pod Lifecycle

| Phase | Description |
|-------|-------------|
| `Pending` | Accepted but not yet scheduled to a node |
| `Running` | Bound to a node, at least one container running |
| `Succeeded` | All containers terminated successfully (exit 0) |
| `Failed` | All containers terminated, at least one failed |
| `Unknown` | Pod state cannot be determined (node communication lost) |

## kubectl Pod Commands

```bash
# List pods
kubectl get pods -n my-namespace
kubectl get pods -l app=my-app -o wide
kubectl get pods --all-namespaces

# Describe (events, conditions, container statuses)
kubectl describe pod my-app -n my-namespace

# Logs
kubectl logs my-app -n my-namespace
kubectl logs my-app -c sidecar --tail=100 -f
kubectl logs my-app --previous  # logs from previous crash

# Exec into a running container
kubectl exec -it my-app -- /bin/sh
kubectl exec -it my-app -c sidecar -- /bin/bash

# Port-forward for local access
kubectl port-forward pod/my-app 8080:8080
kubectl port-forward svc/my-service 3000:80

# Copy files
kubectl cp my-app:/app/logs/error.log ./error.log
kubectl cp ./config.yaml my-app:/app/config.yaml

# Delete
kubectl delete pod my-app
kubectl delete pod my-app --grace-period=0 --force
```

## Resource Requests and Limits

Every container should specify resource requests (scheduling guarantee) and limits (hard ceiling):

```yaml
resources:
  requests:
    cpu: 100m      # 0.1 CPU cores — scheduler uses this
    memory: 128Mi  # 128 MiB — scheduler uses this
  limits:
    cpu: 500m      # 0.5 CPU cores — throttled above this
    memory: 512Mi  # 512 MiB — OOMKilled above this
```

- **CPU** is measured in millicores (1000m = 1 core). Exceeding limits causes throttling, not termination.
- **Memory** is measured in bytes (Mi = mebibytes). Exceeding limits causes OOMKill.
- Set requests = limits for guaranteed QoS. Set requests < limits for burstable QoS.

## Probes

Kubernetes uses three probes to manage container health:

| Probe | Purpose | Failure Action |
|-------|---------|----------------|
| `livenessProbe` | Is the process healthy? | Restart the container |
| `readinessProbe` | Can it accept traffic? | Remove from Service endpoints |
| `startupProbe` | Has it finished starting? | Block liveness/readiness until success |

Probe types: `httpGet`, `tcpSocket`, `exec`, `grpc`.

```yaml
livenessProbe:
  httpGet:
    path: /healthz
    port: 8080
  initialDelaySeconds: 10
  periodSeconds: 15
  failureThreshold: 3

readinessProbe:
  httpGet:
    path: /ready
    port: 8080
  initialDelaySeconds: 5
  periodSeconds: 10

startupProbe:
  httpGet:
    path: /healthz
    port: 8080
  failureThreshold: 30
  periodSeconds: 10
```

## Multi-Container Patterns

### Init Containers

Run to completion before app containers start. Use for setup tasks:

```yaml
spec:
  initContainers:
    - name: init-db
      image: busybox:1.36
      command: ['sh', '-c', 'until nslookup db-service; do echo waiting for db; sleep 2; done']
    - name: init-migrations
      image: myregistry/migrator:latest
      command: ['./migrate', 'up']
  containers:
    - name: app
      image: myregistry/my-app:v1.2.0
```

### Sidecar Containers

Run alongside the main container for cross-cutting concerns:

```yaml
spec:
  containers:
    - name: app
      image: myregistry/my-app:v1.2.0
      ports:
        - containerPort: 8080
    - name: log-shipper
      image: fluent/fluent-bit:latest
      volumeMounts:
        - name: app-logs
          mountPath: /var/log/app
  volumes:
    - name: app-logs
      emptyDir: {}
```

Common sidecars: log shippers, metrics exporters, service mesh proxies (Envoy/Istio), auth proxies.

## Environment Variables

| Variable | Purpose |
|----------|----------|
| `KUBECONFIG` | Path to kubeconfig file |
| `KUBE_NAMESPACE` | Default namespace for kubectl commands |

sections.title: Pod lifecycle phases
sections.body: Pods transition through phases: Pending (scheduling), Running (at least one container up), Succeeded (all exited 0), Failed (at least one exited non-zero), Unknown (node unreachable). Use `kubectl get pods` to see phase and `kubectl describe pod` for detailed conditions and events. ContainerStatuses show Waiting (reason: CrashLoopBackOff, ImagePullBackOff), Running, or Terminated (reason: Completed, OOMKilled, Error).

sections.title: Resource requests and limits
sections.body: Set `resources.requests` for scheduling guarantees (how much CPU/memory the scheduler reserves) and `resources.limits` for hard ceilings (container is throttled or OOMKilled beyond this). CPU in millicores (100m = 0.1 core), memory in MiB/GiB. QoS classes: Guaranteed (requests == limits), Burstable (requests < limits), BestEffort (no requests/limits). Always set at least requests to prevent node overcommit.

sections.title: Health probes
sections.body: Three probe types: livenessProbe (restart on failure), readinessProbe (remove from [[kubernetes/services]] endpoints), startupProbe (gate liveness/readiness during slow startup). Probe methods: httpGet (path + port), tcpSocket (port), exec (command), grpc (port + service). Key fields: initialDelaySeconds, periodSeconds, failureThreshold, successThreshold, timeoutSeconds.

sections.title: Multi-container patterns
sections.body: Init containers run sequentially to completion before app containers start — use for migrations, config generation, and dependency checks. Sidecar containers run alongside the main container for cross-cutting concerns: log shipping, metrics, service mesh proxies, auth proxies. All containers in a Pod share localhost networking and can share volumes via emptyDir or projected volumes.

sections.title: Debugging Pods
sections.body: Debug workflow: 1) `kubectl get pods` — check STATUS column. 2) `kubectl describe pod <name>` — read Events section at bottom. 3) `kubectl logs <name> --previous` — logs from crashed container. 4) `kubectl exec -it <name> -- /bin/sh` — interactive shell. 5) `kubectl port-forward pod/<name> 8080:8080` — local access. Common issues: ImagePullBackOff (wrong image/registry auth), CrashLoopBackOff (app crash — check logs), OOMKilled (increase memory limits), Pending (insufficient resources — check node capacity). See [[kubernetes/troubleshooting]] for deeper diagnosis.

links.context: Services route traffic to Pod endpoints
Inject configuration into Pods via env vars and volumes
Mount persistent storage into Pod containers
Jobs create Pods for batch work
HPA scales Pod replicas based on metrics
Build the container images that Pods run

related.note: Deployments are the standard way to manage Pod replicas — rarely create Pods directly
Services provide stable networking for Pods
Troubleshooting covers advanced Pod debugging techniques
Pods are the Kubernetes equivalent of Docker containers

scripts.name: Get Pod Status
scripts.description: List all pods in a namespace with their status, restarts, and age
scripts.inline:
#!/bin/bash
set -euo pipefail
NS=${KUBE_NAMESPACE:-default}
echo "Pods in namespace: $NS"
kubectl get pods -n "$NS" -o wide
echo ""
echo "Pod events (last 10 minutes):"
kubectl get events -n "$NS" --sort-by=.metadata.creationTimestamp --field-selector type!=Normal | tail -20

scripts.name: Debug Pod
scripts.description: Describe a pod, show recent logs, and display events for debugging
scripts.inline:
#!/bin/bash
set -euo pipefail
POD=${1:?Usage: pod-debug.sh <pod-name>}
NS=${KUBE_NAMESPACE:-default}
echo "=== Pod Description ==="
kubectl describe pod "$POD" -n "$NS"
echo ""
echo "=== Recent Logs (last 50 lines) ==="
kubectl logs "$POD" -n "$NS" --tail=50 2>/dev/null || echo "No logs available"
echo ""
echo "=== Previous Container Logs ==="
kubectl logs "$POD" -n "$NS" --previous --tail=30 2>/dev/null || echo "No previous logs"

containerStatuses.description: Status of each container in the pod

depends.reason: Pods run container images — the image must exist in a registry the cluster can pull from

frontmatter.author: dojo-community
frontmatter.audience: developers, DevOps engineers, SREs, AI agents
